templates.title: 72-Hour Emergency Kit Check
templates.description: Verify your household 72-hour emergency kit is complete, current, and accessible.

items.text: Water: 1 gallon per person per day for 3 days
items.detail: Check containers for leaks and expiration dates.

items.text: Food: 3-day supply of non-perishable items
items.detail: Include a manual can opener if needed.

items.text: Flashlight and extra batteries
First aid kit
items.detail: Check expiration on medications and ointments.

items.text: Battery-powered or hand-crank radio
items.detail: NOAA Weather Radio preferred.

items.text: Phone charger (battery bank or solar)
Whistle to signal for help
Dust masks and plastic sheeting
items.detail: For sheltering in place during air quality events.

items.text: Copies of important documents in waterproof bag
items.detail: ID, insurance, medical records, emergency contacts.

items.text: Cash in small bills
items.detail: ATMs and card readers may be down.

templates.title: Home Power Outage Preparation
templates.description: Pre-storm or pre-outage readiness checklist for your household.

items.text: Charge all phones, tablets, and battery banks
Fill bathtubs and large containers with water
items.detail: For flushing toilets if water pumps lose power.

items.text: Set refrigerator and freezer to coldest setting
items.detail: Keeps food safe longer if power goes out.

items.text: Locate flashlights and lanterns
items.detail: Place one in each occupied room.

items.text: Check propane or fuel for backup heating
items.detail: Never use outdoor heaters or grills indoors.

items.text: Unplug sensitive electronics
items.detail: Prevents damage from power surges when restored.

items.text: Review household meeting point and check-in plan
Verify weather radio has fresh batteries

templates.title: Quarterly Supply Rotation
templates.description: Rotate stored food, water, batteries, and medications every 90 days.

items.text: Check water storage dates and rotate oldest containers
items.detail: Replace water stored longer than 6 months.

items.text: Inspect canned food for damage or bulging
items.detail: Discard any suspect cans safely.

items.text: Replace expired medications and first aid supplies
Test all flashlights and replace batteries
Test battery banks and solar chargers
items.detail: Charge fully and verify output.

items.text: Check fuel levels for generator or camp stove
Update inventory list with current quantities and dates
items.detail: Use the Inventory tab in this app.

templates.title: Family Communication Plan Review
templates.description: Review and update your household emergency communication and meeting plan.

items.text: Verify all household members have emergency contact list
items.detail: Include out-of-area contact person.

items.text: Review primary and secondary meeting locations
items.detail: One near home, one outside neighborhood.

items.text: Practice texting as primary communication method
items.detail: Texts often get through when calls cannot.

items.text: Confirm school and workplace emergency procedures
Review pet evacuation plan
items.detail: Identify pet-friendly shelters nearby.

items.text: Update ICE contacts on all household phones
items.detail: In Case of Emergency contacts should be current.

templates.title: Vehicle Emergency Kit Check
templates.description: Verify your vehicle emergency kit is stocked and accessible.

items.text: Jumper cables or portable jump starter
Reflective triangles or flares
Blanket and warm clothing layer
items.detail: Critical for cold-weather breakdowns.

items.text: Water bottles (at least 1 liter)
items.detail: Replace seasonally to prevent freezing or heat damage.

items.text: Basic first aid kit
Flashlight with extra batteries
Phone charging cable and car adapter
Paper map of local area
items.detail: GPS may not be available in remote areas.

templates.title: Getting Started Household Setup
templates.description: Set up the household basics that make the app faster to use under stress.

items.text: Choose your primary hazards and region
items.detail: Use onboarding or Settings so suggestions match the risks you actually face.

items.text: Add at least one emergency contact
items.detail: Store a local contact so the I'm Safe shortcut is ready before you need it.

items.text: Confirm two meeting locations for your household
items.detail: Pick one near home and one outside the neighborhood.

items.text: Add core inventory items for water, lighting, and first aid
items.detail: A small accurate inventory is more useful than a large incomplete one.

items.text: Pin at least two quick cards or handbook sections
items.detail: Keep your most likely emergency references one tap away from Home.

items.text: Review the 72-hour kit and family communication checklists
items.detail: Use them as your first maintenance loop, then adjust for your household.

templates.title: Adult Hands-Only CPR
templates.description: Reviewed static reference for adult hands-only CPR until EMS or an AED takes over.

items.text: Call 911 and send someone for an AED if one is nearby.
items.detail: Start the emergency response first. Put the phone on speaker if you are alone.

items.text: If the person is unresponsive and not breathing normally, place them flat on a firm surface.
items.detail: Occasional gasps are not normal breathing.

items.text: Place the heel of one hand in the center of the chest and the other hand on top.
items.detail: Lock your elbows and position your shoulders directly over your hands.

items.text: Push hard and fast at about 100 to 120 compressions per minute.
items.detail: Compress the chest about 2 inches deep and let it fully recoil between compressions.

items.text: Keep going until an AED is ready, EMS takes over, or the person clearly starts moving or breathing.
items.detail: Switch compressors if another trained adult is available and you are tiring out.

items.text: If an AED arrives, turn it on and follow the spoken prompts immediately.
items.detail: Resume compressions as soon as the device tells you to.

templates.title: Choking Response
templates.description: Reviewed static steps for a responsive adult or child over age one who cannot speak or breathe.

items.text: Ask if the person is choking and call 911 or direct someone to call.
items.detail: Act fast if they cannot speak, cough, or breathe.

items.text: Stand to the side and slightly behind them. Lean them forward.
items.detail: Support their chest with one hand.

items.text: Give 5 firm back blows between the shoulder blades.
items.detail: Use the heel of your hand and check after each blow.

items.text: If the object does not clear, give 5 abdominal thrusts.
items.detail: Stand behind, place a fist just above the navel, and pull sharply inward and upward.

items.text: Alternate 5 back blows and 5 abdominal thrusts until the object clears or the person becomes unresponsive.
items.detail: Do not stop to offer food or drink.

items.text: If the person becomes unresponsive, lower them to the ground and begin CPR.
items.detail: Use an AED as soon as one is available.

templates.title: Severe Bleeding Response
templates.description: Reviewed static steps to control serious external bleeding while emergency help is on the way.

items.text: Call 911 for heavy, spurting, or uncontrolled bleeding.
items.detail: If possible, put the phone on speaker so you can keep working.

items.text: Press hard directly on the wound with a clean cloth, dressing, or gloved hand.
items.detail: Use both hands if needed. Continuous pressure matters most.

items.text: Do not keep lifting the dressing to check.
items.detail: If blood soaks through, add more material on top and keep pressing.

items.text: If safe and no obvious fracture is present, raise the injured area above the heart while maintaining pressure.
items.detail: Do not move the limb if movement increases pain or appears unsafe.

items.text: Use a tourniquet only for life-threatening bleeding from an arm or leg when direct pressure is not enough and you know how to apply it.
items.detail: Place it above the wound, not over a joint.

items.text: Keep the person still and warm until responders arrive.
items.detail: Watch for signs of shock such as pale skin, weakness, or confusion.

templates.title: Burns Response
templates.description: Reviewed static steps for cooling and protecting a burn after the heat source is removed.

items.text: Stop the burning source and move to a safe area.
items.detail: For electrical or chemical burns, call 911 and follow scene safety first.

items.text: Cool the burn with cool running water for 10 to 20 minutes.
items.detail: Do not use ice or very cold water.

items.text: Remove rings, watches, or tight items before swelling starts if they are not stuck to the skin.
items.detail: Do not peel away clothing that is fused to the burn.

items.text: Cover the burn with a clean, dry, non-fluffy dressing.
items.detail: A sterile dressing or clean cloth is enough.

items.text: Do not pop blisters or apply butter, oils, ointments, or ice.
items.detail: These can trap heat or increase tissue damage.

items.text: Seek urgent care or call 911 for large burns or burns to the face, hands, feet, genitals, major joints, or from electricity or chemicals.
items.detail: Treat breathing trouble after a fire as an emergency.

templates.title: Shock Response
templates.description: Reviewed static steps to support someone who may be going into shock while emergency help is on the way.

items.text: Call 911 if the person has serious injury, chest pain, trouble breathing, major bleeding, or worsening confusion.
items.detail: Shock is a medical emergency.

items.text: Lay the person flat if it is safe to do so.
items.detail: Avoid moving them if you suspect head, neck, back, or major leg injury.

items.text: If there is no suspected spine or leg injury and breathing is okay, raise the legs 6 to 12 inches.
items.detail: Stop if it causes pain or breathing trouble.

items.text: Keep the person warm with a coat or blanket.
items.detail: Do not overheat them and do not use direct heat.

items.text: Do not give food or drink.
items.detail: They may need surgery or may vomit if they lose consciousness.

items.text: Monitor breathing and be ready to begin CPR if they stop breathing normally.
items.detail: Use an AED if available.

templates.title: Severe Allergic Reaction
templates.description: Reviewed static steps for a severe allergic reaction with breathing trouble, swelling, or rapidly worsening symptoms.

items.text: Call 911 for trouble breathing, throat swelling, dizziness, fainting, or rapidly spreading symptoms.
items.detail: Do not wait for symptoms to become severe if anaphylaxis is suspected.

items.text: Use a prescribed epinephrine auto-injector immediately if one is available.
items.detail: Follow the device instructions and inject into the outer thigh.

items.text: Note the time the epinephrine was given.
items.detail: Responders may need to know whether and when a second dose was used.

items.text: Keep the person lying down or seated upright if that makes breathing easier.
items.detail: Do not let them stand or walk around if they feel faint.

items.text: If symptoms continue and medical guidance or device instructions allow, a second dose may be needed after 5 to 15 minutes.
items.detail: Use only if the person has another prescribed device available.

items.text: Even if the person improves, they still need emergency evaluation.
items.detail: Symptoms can return after a short period.

templates.title: Hypothermia Response
templates.description: Reviewed static steps to warm someone safely after dangerous cold exposure.

items.text: Move the person to warm, dry shelter.
items.detail: Get them out of wind, rain, snow, or cold ground as quickly as you safely can.

items.text: Remove wet clothing and replace it with dry layers and blankets.
items.detail: Cover the head and neck as well.

items.text: Warm the center of the body first: chest, neck, groin, and armpits.
items.detail: Use warm packs wrapped in cloth or skin-to-skin body heat if needed.

items.text: Give warm, sweet drinks only if the person is fully awake and can swallow safely.
items.detail: Do not give alcohol.

items.text: Do not rub the arms or legs aggressively and do not use direct high heat.
items.detail: Rapid warming of the extremities can worsen the situation.

items.text: Call 911 for confusion, slurred speech, extreme drowsiness, or unconsciousness.
items.detail: Be ready to start CPR if breathing stops.

templates.title: Heat Illness Response
templates.description: Reviewed static steps for suspected heat exhaustion or heat stroke while cooling the person fast.

items.text: Move the person to shade or air conditioning immediately.
items.detail: Get them off hot pavement or out of a hot vehicle right away.

items.text: Loosen or remove excess clothing.
items.detail: Airflow helps cooling start faster.

items.text: Cool with cool water, wet cloths, a fan, or wrapped ice packs on the neck, armpits, and groin.
items.detail: Use what is available safely and keep cooling continuously.

items.text: Give small sips of cool water only if the person is fully alert and not vomiting.
items.detail: Do not force fluids if they are confused or sleepy.

items.text: Call 911 for confusion, fainting, seizures, hot dry skin, or a temperature over 103 F if known.
items.detail: These are warning signs of heat stroke.

items.text: Stay with the person until they improve or EMS takes over.
items.detail: Watch for worsening confusion or vomiting.

templates.title: Suspected Poisoning Response
templates.description: Reviewed static steps for suspected poisoning, including when to call Poison Help versus 911.

items.text: Call Poison Help at 1-800-222-1222 right away unless the person has collapsed, is having a seizure, has trouble breathing, or cannot wake up.
items.detail: For those emergency signs, call 911 first.

items.text: Remove the source if it is safe and move to fresh air if fumes are involved.
items.detail: Do not put yourself in danger during the move.

items.text: Do not induce vomiting unless Poison Help or a medical professional tells you to.
items.detail: Induced vomiting can make some poisonings worse.

items.text: If poison is on the skin or in the eyes, rinse with clean running water.
items.detail: Keep rinsing while you get more instructions.

items.text: Gather the container, label, amount, and time of exposure if you can.
items.detail: This helps Poison Help and responders guide the next step.

items.text: Follow Poison Help instructions exactly until emergency help arrives or the danger passes.
items.detail: Do not give food, drink, or home remedies unless instructed.

templates.title: Fracture Or Severe Sprain Response
templates.description: Reviewed static steps to protect and cool a suspected fracture or severe sprain until care is available.

items.text: Stop activity and protect the injured area immediately.
items.detail: Do not test the injury by having the person walk or move it repeatedly.

items.text: Call 911 for obvious deformity, heavy bleeding, numbness, bluish skin, or suspected spine, hip, or major limb injury.
items.detail: Treat loss of pulse or severe swelling as urgent.

items.text: Immobilize the area in the position found.
items.detail: Do not try to straighten a visibly deformed limb.

items.text: Apply a wrapped cold pack for 15 to 20 minutes at a time.
items.detail: Do not place ice directly on bare skin.

items.text: Elevate the injured area if possible without causing more pain.
items.detail: Stop if movement increases pain or feels unsafe.

items.text: Do not give food or drink if emergency surgery might be needed.
items.detail: Keep the person warm and still while you wait for care.
